title: Test Nginx Before Reload
hook: The config looked fine. Nginx disagreed before reload broke anything.
problem: You changed an Nginx config and need to validate syntax before reloading the service.
starting_state: A server block exists and may have been edited.
what_changed: Nothing changes. Nginx parses configuration and reports whether reload is safe.
when_to_use: Use this before any Nginx reload or deploy involving server blocks.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat a syntax pass as proof the upstream app or DNS is correct.
expected_output: A syntax-ok message or a line number for the config error.

shorts.title: Test Nginx before reload.
shorts.voiceover: Before reloading Nginx, run the syntax check. It is faster than breaking a live site.
shorts.caption: Run `nginx -t` before reload.

linkedin.question: Do you run `nginx -t` before every reload?

youtube.description: A quick Nginx safety check before reloading a web server.

experiments.variant_b: The config looked fine. Nginx disagreed.

title: Show Enabled Nginx Sites
hook: The config existed, but it was not enabled.
problem: You need to see which Nginx site configs are actually enabled.
starting_state: Nginx has available and enabled site directories.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists enabled config symlinks.
when_to_use: Use this when a server block edit seems ignored.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete symlinks from this list unless you know the site should be disabled.
expected_output: Enabled site config filenames or symlink targets.

shorts.title: Is the Nginx site enabled?
shorts.voiceover: Editing a config is not enough. Check whether Nginx actually has that site enabled.
shorts.caption: Enabled sites are what Nginx reads.

linkedin.question: Have you ever edited the available site instead of the enabled one?

youtube.description: How to list enabled Nginx site configs from the terminal.

experiments.variant_b: The config existed, but it was not enabled.

title: Find Which Nginx Config Owns a Domain
hook: The wrong server block was answering the domain.
problem: You need to find which Nginx config contains a domain's `server_name`.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command searches enabled server names.
when_to_use: Use this when a domain routes to the wrong site.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume the first match controls HTTPS if separate SSL configs exist.

shorts.title: Find the Nginx config for a domain.
shorts.voiceover: When the wrong site answers, search the enabled server names before editing random files.
shorts.caption: Find the server block before changing it.

linkedin.question: What do you check first when Nginx serves the wrong site?

youtube.description: A quick grep for finding which Nginx config owns a domain.

experiments.variant_a: Find which Nginx config owns a domain.
experiments.variant_b: The wrong server block was answering.

title: Check HTTP to HTTPS Redirect
hook: HTTPS worked. The plain HTTP redirect still mattered.
problem: You need to confirm that plain HTTP redirects to HTTPS.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command requests headers from HTTP.
when_to_use: Use this after SSL setup, DNS changes, or Nginx redirect edits.
when_not_to_use: Do not follow redirects if you only need the first response; keep `-I` simple.
expected_output: A 301 or 308 redirect with a HTTPS Location header.

shorts.title: Check the HTTP redirect.
shorts.voiceover: A valid cert is not the whole setup. Check the plain HTTP path too.
shorts.caption: HTTP should point to HTTPS.

linkedin.question: Do you test HTTP redirects after setting up SSL?

youtube.description: How to check HTTP to HTTPS redirects with curl.

title: Inspect Response Headers
hook: The page loaded, but the headers told the operational story.
problem: You need to inspect server and security headers quickly.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints response headers only.
when_to_use: Use this to check redirects, server headers, cache headers, and security headers.
when_not_to_use: Do not use headers alone to validate page content or application behavior.

shorts.title: Inspect headers before guessing.
shorts.voiceover: A page can load while headers reveal redirect, cache, or security problems.
shorts.caption: Headers are fast operational clues.

linkedin.question: Which header do you check first on a new site?

youtube.description: A curl command for inspecting site response headers.

experiments.variant_b: The page loaded, but the headers told the story.

title: Check a Domain A Record
hook: The site was fine. The domain was pointed somewhere else.
problem: You need to see the IPv4 address a domain currently resolves to.
starting_state: A domain should point to a server IP.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command queries DNS.
when_to_use: Use this during DNS cutovers, migrations, and stale resolver debugging.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume one resolver represents every user during DNS propagation.

shorts.title: Check where the domain points.
shorts.voiceover: Before blaming the server, check the A record. The domain may be pointing somewhere else.
shorts.caption: DNS first, then server.

linkedin.question: When a site disappears, do you check DNS before Nginx?

youtube.description: How to check a domain A record from the Linux terminal.

experiments.variant_b: The site was fine. DNS pointed elsewhere.

title: List Certbot Certificates
hook: The certificate existed. The question was which domains it covered.
problem: You need to see Certbot-managed certificates and included domains.
starting_state: A server may have one or more Certbot certificates.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints certificate inventory.
when_to_use: Use this before renewing, expanding, or debugging Let's Encrypt certificates.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume public DNS is correct just because a certificate exists locally.
expected_output: Certificate names, domains, and expiry details.

shorts.title: List the certs before changing SSL.
shorts.voiceover: Before editing SSL config, check what Certbot already knows about the domain.
shorts.caption: Inventory certificates first.

linkedin.question: Do you check Certbot inventory before changing SSL config?

youtube.description: How to list Certbot certificates and covered domains.

experiments.variant_b: The cert existed. Which domains did it cover?

title: Check the Current Release Symlink
hook: The deploy finished. The symlink told me what was actually live.
problem: You need to verify which release directory a site is serving.
starting_state: A site deploy uses timestamped releases and a `current` symlink.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command resolves the live symlink target.
when_to_use: Use this after a deploy or rollback.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete old releases until you know rollback requirements.
expected_output: The absolute path of the active release directory.

shorts.title: Which release is live?
shorts.voiceover: If deploys use a current symlink, resolve it before debugging the wrong release.
shorts.caption: Check the live release target.

linkedin.question: Do your deploys expose a current symlink or release marker?

youtube.description: How to check which release directory a Linux site is serving.

experiments.variant_b: The symlink told me what was actually live.

title: Find Top 404 URLs
hook: The missing file was not random. The access log had a pattern.
problem: You need to see which URLs are producing 404 responses.
starting_state: Nginx access logs contain status codes and request paths.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command counts missing paths.
when_to_use: Use this after deploys, migrations, or SEO cleanup.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume every 404 is a bug; scanners and bots create noise.

shorts.title: Find the URLs throwing 404s.
shorts.voiceover: One missing asset can show up again and again. Count 404 paths before guessing.
shorts.caption: Rank your 404s from the access log.

youtube.description: A one-liner for finding top 404 URLs in Nginx logs.

experiments.variant_b: The access log had a pattern.

title: See Top Referrers
hook: LinkedIn traffic was not a guess. The referrer field showed it.
problem: You need a rough look at which referrers are sending requests.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command counts referrer strings.
when_to_use: Use this as a quick server-side sanity check for campaign traffic.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat referrers as complete analytics; some clients strip or spoof them.

shorts.title: Check referrers from access logs.
shorts.voiceover: Platform analytics can be partial. Your server logs can still show referrer clues.
shorts.caption: Server-side referrer sanity check.

linkedin.question: Do you trust platform analytics, server logs, or both?

youtube.description: A quick command for counting referrers in Nginx access logs.

experiments.variant_b: LinkedIn traffic was not a guess.
